title: Vermis, by Plastiboo - a book so good I don't want to finish it
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oPCHIAxcTHw

Ambient musicians joke that they write music for films that don't exist; Daniel (@dungeondive) presents Vermis by Plastiboo as the tabletop equivalent — a sourcebook for a game that doesn't exist. Published by Hollow Press, it's a guidebook to an imaginary dark-fantasy video game, and Daniel is savouring it so slowly he admits he doesn't want it to end. The tour is irresistible: character classes like the Mad Pricker, the Stone Scholar (blinded by rocks tied to their faces for ages), the Rat Man, and the Cursed Fool, each with stats for rules that were never written; a pantheon; then a travelogue through Greengrave and the Popper's Catacombs written like a choose-your-own-adventure without choices — complete with a Lonely Knight guarding a coffin whose dialogue, Daniel notes, ends with the tell-tale Dark Souls chuckle. His dream scenario: hand FromSoftware the book and commission Dark Souls 4. There's even a bestiary of skeletal horrors he christens 'Bone Thugs and Harmony' (E. 1999 Eternal being, he confides, his favourite hip-hop album of all time). Filed somewhere between weird fiction and Mörk Borg world-building fuel, Vermis earns its place among his treasured volumes — pure world, no stats, endlessly generative.

----

Vermis is lore with no game attached — and Daniel wants to savour rather than finish it. What book, game manual, or artbook have you deliberately rationed because ending it felt like a loss?
